skill.name: benchmark-kernel
skill.description: Guides users through benchmarking FlashInfer GPU kernels with CUPTI or CUDA event timing.
skill.summary: Benchmark FlashInfer attention, GEMM, and MOE kernels with reproducible GPU timing.
skill.author: flashinfer-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command patterns, but review found they are Markdown benchmark examples, package installation commands, and command-line flags in SKILL.md. One sudo nvidia-smi command is a real privileged operation for GPU clock control, so publication is acceptable with a warning. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, obfuscation, or malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Privileged GPU Clock Command
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md recommends sudo nvidia-smi -lgc for disabling GPU boost during advanced benchmarking. This is a legitimate performance-tuning command, but it requires elevated privileges and can change host GPU clock behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo command is explicit and would run with administrator privileges if copied by a user. The surrounding troubleshooting context is legitimate benchmarking guidance, so this is risky but not malicious.

medium_findings.title: Local Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill contains many shell examples for installing CUPTI, running FlashInfer benchmark scripts, writing CSV output, and generating reproducer commands. These are expected for a benchmarking skill, but users should inspect parameters before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible Markdown examples and align with the skill purpose. They still execute local Python scripts or install packages, which carries operational risk in user environments.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Matches
low_findings.description: Static findings for weak cryptographic algorithms at SKILL.md lines 3 and 148 do not correspond to cryptographic code. Line 3 is the skill description, and line 148 starts a Markdown table of benchmark flags.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of both locations found descriptive Markdown only. No hash, cipher, or cryptographic implementation appears at those lines.

low_findings.title: False Positive Network Reconnaissance Match
low_findings.description: The static network reconnaissance finding at SKILL.md line 131 is a benchmark argument line for FP8 GEMM dimensions, not a network scan or host discovery command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 131 is inside a benchmark test list and contains matrix dimensions and dtype flags. No network target, scanner, or reconnaissance behavior is present.

low_findings.title: Backtick Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections are Markdown inline code and fenced code blocks used for documentation. They do not execute automatically when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The detected backticks are Markdown formatting around package names, flags, commands, and output examples. The file is documentation and contains no executable Ruby code.

dangerous_patterns.title: sudo nvidia-smi Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents a sudo nvidia-smi command for locking GPU clocks. Users should run it only on machines where they are authorized to change GPU settings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly present in a shell block. Its purpose is legitimate, but it can alter system-level GPU configuration.

dangerous_patterns.title: pip Package Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends pip install -U cupti-python. Package installation is normal for this workflow, but users should verify package trust and environment isolation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The package install command is explicit and repeated. It installs a public dependency needed for CUPTI timing, not an unexplained payload.

content.value_statement: GPU kernel benchmarks can vary because timing methods, warmups, and backend settings differ. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through repeatable FlashInfer benchmarks with CUPTI or CUDA events.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use CUPTI timing and when CUDA events are an acceptable fallback.
Provides command examples for FlashInfer attention, GEMM, and MOE benchmark routines.
Shows how to run single benchmarks and batch benchmark lists with CSV output.
Describes key benchmark metrics including median time, standard deviation, TFLOPS, and bandwidth.
Includes Python examples for using bench_gpu_time in custom kernel scripts.
Offers troubleshooting steps for CUPTI warnings, inconsistent results, and backend mismatch errors.
limitations: Requires a working FlashInfer checkout with benchmark scripts already available.
Assumes access to CUDA-capable NVIDIA hardware for meaningful results.
Some commands may need CUDA 13, CUPTI, or privileged GPU clock control.
Does not validate benchmark correctness beyond the documented refcheck workflow.

use_cases.title: Compare Attention Backends
use_cases.description: Run consistent decode or prefill attention benchmarks across FlashAttention, cuDNN, CUTLASS, and TensorRT-LLM backends.

use_cases.title: Measure Custom Kernel Time
use_cases.description: Use bench_gpu_time to measure a custom CUDA kernel wrapper with warmups, repeated iterations, and optional CUPTI timing.

use_cases.title: Create Reproducible Benchmark Tables
use_cases.description: Save benchmark results to CSV and generate reproducer commands for sharing performance results with a team.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Basic Benchmark
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user wants one benchmark command.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the benchmark-kernel skill to help me run one FlashInfer decode attention benchmark with safe defaults and refcheck enabled.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Backends
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs a fair backend comparison.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the benchmark-kernel skill to build a FlashInfer benchmark command that compares fa2, fa3, cudnn, and cutlass for my attention shape.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Benchmark Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants repeatable CSV output for several shapes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the benchmark-kernel skill to design a test list and CSV workflow for multiple batch sizes and sequence lengths.

prompt_templates.title: Custom Kernel Timing Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user has Python benchmark code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the benchmark-kernel skill to review my bench_gpu_time setup for warmups, CUPTI fallback, cold cache behavior, and metric reporting.

output_examples.input: Benchmark decode attention on H100 and compare three backends.
output: A ready-to-run benchmark command with routine, backend, shape, dtype, warmup, iteration, and refcheck settings.
A short explanation of the expected median time, variation, TFLOPS, and memory bandwidth fields.
Notes about using CUPTI when available and falling back to CUDA events when it is not installed.

output_examples.input: Create a batch benchmark workflow for GEMM and attention cases.
output: A compact benchmark list structure covering the requested routines and shape variations.
A command that saves results to CSV and generates reproducer commands.
Guidance on increasing warmup or measurement iterations when results vary too much.

output_examples.input: Review my custom bench_gpu_time script for a new kernel.
output: A checklist for input tensor placement, warmup count, iteration count, CUPTI setting, and fallback behavior.
Suggestions for reporting median time, standard deviation, and derived throughput.
Warnings about privileged GPU clock changes and environment-specific requirements.

best_practices: Use refcheck when comparing backends so performance results do not hide output mismatches.
Run enough warmup and measurement iterations before comparing median time across backends.
Save CSV results and reproducer commands when sharing benchmark conclusions.
anti_patterns: Do not compare backends with different shapes, dtypes, or sequence lengths.
Do not treat one run as final when standard deviation is high.
Do not run privileged GPU clock commands without authorization and rollback planning.

faq.question: Does this skill run benchmarks automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and review guidance that a user can choose to run in a FlashInfer environment.

faq.question: Is CUPTI required?
faq.answer: No. CUPTI is preferred for accurate GPU timing, but the guide explains CUDA event fallback behavior.

faq.question: What hardware is needed?
faq.answer: Meaningful results require NVIDIA GPU hardware with CUDA support and compatible FlashInfer benchmark dependencies.

faq.question: Can it compare multiple backends?
faq.answer: Yes. The guide includes examples for comparing attention, GEMM, and MOE backends with common benchmark flags.

faq.question: Can it produce CSV results?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents the output_path workflow for saving benchmark metrics and reproducer commands.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The skill includes local command examples and one sudo GPU clock command, so users should review commands before running them.
